papers.title: How to Read a Paper
papers.abstract: Researchers spend a great deal of time reading research papers. However, this skill is rarely taught, leading to much wasted effort. This article outlines a practical and efficient three-pass method for reading research papers. I also describe how to use this method to do a literature survey.
papers.notes:
as a undergrad student (2026), i can clearly say that this was all i was looking for. 

Apr 26 2026, i suddenly googled as "how to read a paper" an this was what I faced with.

To conclude, this paper suggests a three-pass approach to read articles for more efficient way.

pass one -> quickly skim the headings, abstraction and conclusion. approx. 10-15min

pass two -> read more deeply, but do not interfere with proofs if it is a technical paper;mark relevant unread references for further reading. pay attention to figures. approx. 1hr

pass three -> basically re-implement the paper. you need to think as author and brainstorm about the complex structures and criticize the limitations. approx. 4-5 hr

papers.title: Topological turning points across the human lifespa
papers.abstract: Structural topology develops non-linearly across the lifespan and is strongly related to cognitive trajectories. We gathered diffusion imaging from datasets with a collective age range of zero to 90 years old (N = 4,216). We analyzed how 12 graph theory metrics of organization change with age and projected these data into manifold spaces using Uniform Manifold Projection and Approximation. With these manifolds, we identified four major topological turning points across the lifespan – around nine, 32, 66, and 83 years old. These ages defined five major epochs of topological development, each with distinctive age-related changes in topology. These lifespan epochs each have a distinct direction of topological development and specific changes in the organizational properties driving the age-topology relationship. This study underscores the complex, non-linear nature of human development, with unique phases of topological maturation, which can only be illuminated with a multivariate, lifespan, population-level perspective.
papers.notes:
"With these manifolds, we identified four major topological turning points across the lifespan – around nine, 32, 66, and 83 years old"

these 4 major topology changes tell us that our brain development persists in connecting new synapses until the age of 32. From 32 to 66, it reaches a plateau. i keep thinking about this article's philosophical perspective on aging. the study shows that the government classifies people under 18 as children, but this is not the case. between the ages of 9 and 32, an 18-year-old and a 28-year-old exhibit an approximately similar brain structure.

to be honest, i am a little bit relieved. this is because, i do not feel "smart" or "sharp-minded" enough these days.

papers.title: Variational quantum algorithms
papers.abstract:
VQAs have already been considered for a plethora of
applications (see Figure 3), covering essentially all of the applications that researchers had envisioned for quantum computers. Although they may be the key to obtaining near-term quantum advantage, VQAs still face important challenges, including their trainability, accuracy,and efficiency.In this Review, we discuss the exciting prospects for VQAs, and we highlight the challenges that must be overcome to obtain the ultimate goal of quantum advantage.
papers.notes: this paper essentially reviews and consolidates a comprehensive collection of literature on variational quantum algorithms (vqas) into a single framework. the core architecture of a vqa consists of three key components: an ansatz, which is a parameterized quantum circuit used to represent trainable parameters; a cost function that maps quantum states to a high-dimensional landscape that needs to be optimized; and classical optimizers that use gradients to minimize this cost function. however, a critical challenge in training vqas is the phenomenon of barren plateaus, which occurs when gradients vanish and become exponentially close to zero, rendering the optimization landscape flat and making the circuit untrainable.
